title: The Complete Signal Chain: Oscillator to Display
narrationScript: Three lessons gave you the heavy machinery of an eddy current instrument — amplification, phase detection, and filtering. This capstone assembles the whole chain. One oscillator does two jobs: it drives the coil at the test frequency, and it hands the phase-sensitive detector the reference that makes phase measurement possible at all. Between coil and amplifier sits the balance network. A small crack changes the coil voltage by well under one percent, so the instrument first nulls the large standing signal — otherwise gain and converter range are wasted re-amplifying a number that never changes. After demodulation and filtering, the analog-to-digital converter samples the signal. Sample rate plus scan speed set a hard speed limit: scan too fast and a crack signal falls between samples and reads low, with no warning on the screen. Bit depth sets dynamic range, roughly six decibels per bit. Displays, gates, and markers turn the result into decisions. Prove the chain end to end — run the reference standard at production speed, and re-standardize after any change.

sections.heading: The Instrument Is a Chain
sections.body:
An eddy current instrument is a **chain of six links**: an **oscillator** that drives the coil at the test frequency, a **balance network** that nulls the coil's standing signal, an **amplifier** that raises what remains, a **phase-sensitive detector** that resolves the signal into horizontal and vertical components, **filters** that pass flaw rates and reject noise rates, and a **digitizer feeding the display, gates, and outputs**.

The last three lessons took the middle links apart one at a time — amplification, phase detection, differentiation and filtering. This capstone installs the remaining links and forces the system view, because a flaw signal must survive **every link** to reach the screen:

- the **oscillator** — where frequency and drive level are actually executed,
- the **balance network** — why nulling comes before gain,
- the **digitizer** — how sample rate and bit depth set a hard speed limit,
- the **display, gates, and outputs** — where signals become decisions.

A chain is only as strong as its weakest link. A perfectly chosen frequency cannot rescue an undersampled scan, and a razor-sharp filter cannot recover a signal the amplifier already clipped. Level II means you can name the link that failed.

sections.caption: The complete signal chain of an eddy current instrument. One oscillator drives the coil and supplies the phase reference to the detector; the balance network nulls the standing signal before any gain is applied.

sections.heading: One Oscillator, Two Jobs
sections.body:
The oscillator generates the sinusoidal excitation that drives the coil — so every frequency decision you have made in this course (skin depth, phase spread, response speed) is **executed here**. Its second job is just as important: it supplies the **reference signal** the phase-sensitive detector compares against. The phase angle of the returning coil signal only means something when it is measured against the same wave that created it — one clock for drive and detection is what makes the impedance plane readable.

Two design consequences follow:

- **Stability.** Your standardized phase angles — lift-off horizontal, notch responses at their recorded angles — assume the frequency holds. Oscillator drift shifts skin depth and phase lag, and the display quietly stops matching the standardization.
- **Drive level.** More drive means stronger eddy currents and a better signal-to-noise ratio, but probe heating sets one limit, and on ferromagnetic parts a strong field pushes permeability into its nonlinear range and distorts the signal — the field-strength effects you studied in the coupling module.

**Multifrequency instruments** run two or more frequencies through the coil simultaneously or time-multiplexed, each with its own detection channel — the hardware basis for the signal mixing used in tubing inspection to suppress support-plate responses.

sections.heading: Balance: Null the Signal That Never Changes
sections.body:
Put magnitudes on the problem. The coil sitting on defect-free material returns a large standing voltage. A small fatigue crack changes that voltage by **well under one percent** — the information you are paid to find is a tiny ripple riding a large constant.

The classic solution is an **AC bridge**: the test coil forms one arm, and a reference coil or adjustable impedance forms the opposing arm. Balanced, the bridge output is **zero** — the standing signal cancels itself — and any impedance change unbalances the bridge, producing an output proportional to the **change alone**. Modern instruments automate this: press **null**, and the instrument measures the residual signal and stores an equal-and-opposite offset, dropping the operating point to the **origin of the impedance plane**. Everything you read on the screen is measured from that null.

Null does not hold forever:

- **Temperature** changes the copper winding's resistance by roughly **0.4% per °C**, so the dot wanders as the probe and part warm — give the instrument its warm-up time.
- **Cable flex** shifts capacitance and can walk the null mid-scan.
- **Differential probes** hold balance far better, because both coils see slow common-mode changes equally and the bridge stays nulled.

The discipline: null at the operating point your procedure names — on the reference standard, off the flaw — and re-null after any change of probe, cable, frequency, or whenever the dot has drifted.

sections.title: The dynamic-range budget
sections.body: Every stage has finite headroom — the amplifier clips, the converter runs out of counts. An un-nulled standing signal spends that headroom on a number that **never changes**, leaving less for the flaw signal that does. **Balance first. Then amplify. Then digitize.** Every decibel wasted re-amplifying a constant is a decibel unavailable where the crack needs it.

sections.heading: Digitization Sets the Speed Limit
sections.body:
After demodulation and filtering, an **analog-to-digital converter** samples the horizontal and vertical channels. Two numbers define it: **sample rate** and **bit depth**.

Work the sample-rate problem on a rotating bolt-hole scanner. At **2,400 rpm** in a 6.35 mm hole, the circumference is about 20 mm and the coil sweeps the surface at roughly **0.8 m/s**. A 1 mm coil crossing a tight crack interacts over about 2 mm of travel, so the crack signal lasts about **2.5 ms**, once per revolution. A digitizer sampling at **10,000 samples per second** puts about **25 samples** across that signal — the peak is captured faithfully. Sample at 500 per second and roughly **one sample** lands on the event: the same crack reads full amplitude, half amplitude, or nothing, depending on where the samples happen to fall. A practical design target is on the order of **ten samples across the narrowest expected flaw response**.

**Bit depth** sets amplitude resolution — each bit is worth about **6 dB** of range, so a 12-bit converter spans roughly **72 dB** and a 16-bit converter about **96 dB**. A signal living in the bottom few counts comes out coarse and steppy, which is why gain staging still matters in a digital instrument.

The operational consequence: the **maximum scan or rotation speed in your procedure is an instrument-design number**, not a comfort preference. Exceed it and flaws are undersampled — and the screen gives no warning that it happened.

sections.caption: The same 2.5 ms crack signal sampled densely and sparsely. With ~25 samples on the signal the peak is captured; with ~1 sample the recorded amplitude depends on luck and reads low.

sections.heading: Displays, Gates, and Outputs: Where the Chain Pays Off
sections.body:
The end of the chain converts clean signals into decisions, and the format is matched to the technique:

- **Impedance-plane (X–Y) display** — preserves phase, the Level II interpretation workhorse for surface scanning and aerospace structure.
- **Sweep (timebase) display** — one channel plotted against time; on a rotating scanner a crack repeats as a blip at the same angular position every revolution.
- **Strip chart and C-scan** — position-stamped records for tube pulls and automated scanning.

**Alarm gates** automate the first decision. A **box gate** is a pure amplitude window on a channel. A **sector gate**, anchored at the null point, adds **phase**: the wedge is set to exclude the lift-off direction, so probe wobble does not alarm but signals breaking toward the flaw plane do. Gate outputs drive horns, lights, paint markers, and sorter mechanisms — and on a mill line the marker sits downstream of the coil, so the instrument delays the fire command by the transport time computed from line speed.

Finally, prove the chain **end to end**. Dynamic standardization — running the reference standard through the system **at production speed** — is required by tubular-product standards such as ASTM E309 precisely because it exercises every link at once: drive, balance, gain, demodulation, filters, sampling, and gates. Re-standardize after **any** change to the chain — frequency, drive, null, gain, rotation, filters, or speed — and at the intervals your procedure sets. If a re-check fails, everything tested since the last good check is suspect.

sections.caption: One chain, six links — the design parameter each link owns, and what you get when it is set wrong.
rows: Test frequency, drive level, detector reference
Wrong depth of penetration; phase angles drift off standardization
Standing signal eats headroom; dot pinned off-screen; early saturation
Large signals clipped, or small flaws buried in electronic noise
Lift-off not horizontal; phase readings disagree with the standard
Pass band matched to scan speed
Flaw responses removed — or noise passed — when speed changes
Sample rate, bit depth, gate geometry
Undersampled cracks read low; gates false-call on wobble or miss flaws

figures.title: The complete eddy current instrument signal chain

interactive.title: One Chain, One Job Each
interactive.intro: The block diagram below is the instrument you standardize every shift. A small fatigue crack changes the coil voltage by well under one percent — the rest is a large standing signal that never changes. Click the block whose job is to remove that standing signal before anything is amplified.

regions.label: Oscillator
regions.feedback: The oscillator generates the drive at the test frequency and the detector's phase reference — it creates the standing signal; it cannot remove it.

regions.label: Coil + balance network
regions.feedback: Correct. The balance network nulls the standing coil voltage — bridge cancellation or a stored auto-null offset — so the amplifier and digitizer spend their range on changes, not on a constant.

regions.label: Amplifier
regions.feedback: The amplifier boosts whatever reaches it. Un-nulled, the standing signal saturates the amplifier long before the flaw signal reaches useful size.

regions.label: Phase-sensitive detector
regions.feedback: The detector resolves the amplified signal into horizontal and vertical components against the oscillator reference — it measures the signal; it does not null it.

regions.label: Filters + digitizer
regions.feedback: A high-pass filter can block a constant on the demodulated channel, but by then the damage is done — an un-nulled standing signal saturates the amplifier upstream. Balance must happen at the coil, before gain.

checkQuestions.question: Besides driving the test coil, the instrument's oscillator also supplies:
options: the alarm-gate threshold voltage
the high-pass filter corner frequency
the reference signal the phase-sensitive detector compares against
the digitizer's bit depth
checkQuestions.explanation: Phase only has meaning relative to the wave that created the eddy currents. The same oscillator that drives the coil feeds the phase-sensitive detector its reference — one clock for drive and detection is what makes the impedance plane readable.

checkQuestions.question: The purpose of the balance (null) network is to:
options: subtract the coil's large standing voltage so later stages amplify and digitize only the changes
rotate the lift-off response to the horizontal axis
remove high-frequency electronic noise from the signal
increase the depth of penetration at the chosen frequency
checkQuestions.explanation: A flaw changes the coil voltage by well under one percent. Nulling the standing signal — by bridge cancellation or a stored offset — frees the amplifier's headroom and the converter's counts for the part that actually carries information. Rotation belongs to the phase detector, noise rejection to the filters.

checkQuestions.question: A 12-bit analog-to-digital converter gives a theoretical dynamic range of about:
options: 12 dB
24 dB
48 dB
72 dB
checkQuestions.explanation: Each bit of resolution is worth about 6 dB, so 12 bits spans roughly 72 dB and 16 bits about 96 dB. Signals forced into the bottom few counts come out coarse, which is why gain staging still matters in digital instruments.

checkQuestions.question: A bolt-hole crack signal lasts about 2.5 ms at the procedure's rotation speed, but the operator doubles the rpm. The most likely result is that the crack signal:
options: saturates the amplifier
falls between digitizer samples and reads low — or is missed — with no warning on screen
rotates from the flaw plane onto the lift-off axis
causes the oscillator frequency to drift
checkQuestions.explanation: Doubling speed halves the signal duration, so fewer samples land on the event. An undersampled flaw reads at whatever amplitude the nearest samples happened to catch — often low, sometimes zero — and the display gives no indication that sampling failed. Scan-speed limits are instrument-design numbers.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a simple amplitude (box) gate, a sector gate set around the flaw direction reduces false alarms because it:
options: samples the signal at a higher rate
adds gain only to vertical signals
uses phase as well as amplitude, so responses along the lift-off direction do not trigger it
filters out 50/60 Hz line interference
checkQuestions.explanation: A sector gate is anchored at the null point and opens a phase wedge that excludes the lift-off direction. Probe wobble — large in amplitude but lying along lift-off — stays outside the gate, while signals breaking toward the flaw plane alarm.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ECT Level II Principles Course §7.0 (instrument design considerations)
ASNT Recommended Practice No. SNT-TC-1A (2024), Personnel Qualification and Certification in Nondestructive Testing
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (electromagnetic testing terms)
ASTM E309, Standard Practice for Eddy Current Examination of Steel Tubular Products Using Magnetic Saturation (dynamic standardization at production speed)
